title: Pool deck cost in stamped concrete: 2026 pricing guide
excerpt: A stamped concrete pool deck costs $10-$22 per square foot installed in 2026 — typically $6,000-$13,000 for a 600 sq ft surround. Cool-deck adds $1-$3.
metaKeywords: pool deck price per square foot
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed pool-deck, patio, driveway, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a stamped concrete pool deck runs $10-$22 per square foot installed nationally. On a typical 600-square-foot pool surround that's $6,000-$13,200, with most quotes landing between $7,500-$10,500. Standard broom-finish concrete pool decks are cheaper at $7-$12 per square foot but lack the texture-and-color value most homeowners want at a pool. Cool-deck coatings (acrylic cement-based topcoats that stay 20-30 degrees cooler underfoot) add $1-$3 per square foot over the stamped baseline. Pool decks carry a higher base-prep cost than patios because they often integrate with pool coping, require expansion joints at the pool edge, and need stricter drainage to direct water away from the pool shell.

faqs.question: How much does a stamped concrete pool deck cost in 2026?
faqs.answer: A stamped concrete pool deck runs $10-$22 per square foot installed in 2026. On a typical 600-square-foot pool surround (4 feet around an 18 x 36 ft rectangular pool), that's $6,000-$13,200. Most quotes come in at $7,500-$10,500. Custom patterns, multi-color hand-finishing, or cool-deck add-ons push to the upper end of the range.

faqs.question: How much cheaper is broom finish vs stamped for a pool deck?
faqs.answer: Broom-finish concrete pool decks run $7-$12 per square foot installed in 2026, vs $10-$22 for stamped. On a 600-square-foot deck, broom finish is $4,200-$7,200 — typically $2,000-$5,000 cheaper than stamped. The structural slab spec is identical; you're saving on color, pattern, and finish labor by going broom.

faqs.question: What is cool deck and how much does it add?
faqs.answer: Cool deck is an acrylic-cement topcoat troweled or sprayed onto cured concrete that reflects sunlight and stays 20-30 degrees cooler than uncoated concrete. Cost: $3-$7 per square foot as standalone application, $1-$3 added when applied at the time of the original stamped pour. According to research cited by the Portland Cement Association, light-colored cool-deck surfaces reach 105-110°F in full sun vs 135-145°F for standard gray concrete.

faqs.question: Should a pool deck be reinforced?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pool decks are typically reinforced with either #3 rebar on 18-inch centers (recommended) or 6x6 W2.9 wire mesh (acceptable for foot-only traffic). The pool edge and any cantilevered coping require specific reinforcement that's tied into the pool shell. The American Concrete Institute publishes guidance on slab-on-grade design that most pool deck contractors follow.

faqs.question: How much does pool deck resurfacing cost?
faqs.answer: Resurfacing an existing pool deck (cleaning, repair, then applying a stamped overlay or cool-deck topcoat) runs $4-$10 per square foot in 2026 — typically $2,400-$6,000 for a 600 sq ft deck. That's 30-60 percent cheaper than full demo and replacement. Resurfacing only works if the underlying slab is structurally sound — settling, major cracks, or base failure require replacement, not resurfacing.

faqs.question: What kind of finish is best for a pool deck?
faqs.answer: Best pool deck finishes prioritize three things: slip resistance, heat resistance, and durability. Top choices: stamped concrete with a texture-pattern finish (slip-rated through the texture itself), broom finish (highest slip resistance, lowest cost), and acrylic cool-deck overlay (heat-resistant). Polished or smooth-troweled finishes are not recommended at pools because they get slippery when wet.

faqs.question: How long does a stamped concrete pool deck last?
faqs.answer: A properly installed stamped concrete pool deck lasts 25-30 years before requiring resurfacing or replacement. The slab itself can last 50+ years; the stamped color and sealer fade and wear under UV, chlorine, and foot traffic faster than non-pool stamped surfaces. Reseal every 2-3 years to maintain color depth. Cool-deck topcoats typically need recoating every 5-8 years.

faqs.question: Does a pool deck need a permit?
faqs.answer: A standalone pool deck addition typically requires a building permit ($50-$300) and may trigger a setback or drainage review. Decks tied to a new pool installation are usually permitted under the pool permit. The U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration drainage guidance is often referenced in municipal codes requiring pool decks to direct runoff away from the pool, surrounding structures, and neighboring properties.

howToSteps.name: Site prep and excavation
howToSteps.text: The pool deck footprint is marked and excavated to 7-8 inches (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Any existing deck material is removed and hauled off. Adjacent landscaping and the pool itself are protected with plastic and plywood.

howToSteps.name: Base preparation and forms
howToSteps.text: The subgrade is compacted with a plate compactor and a 4-inch compacted gravel base is installed. Forms are set to mark the deck edges with the proper slope (1-2 percent away from the pool). Expansion joint material is placed where the deck meets the pool coping.

howToSteps.name: Place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: Rebar (#3 on 18-inch centers recommended) or wire mesh is placed mid-slab on chairs. Reinforcement at the pool edge is tied carefully to keep the slab from cracking at the edge under thermal cycling.

howToSteps.name: Pour the slab and apply color
howToSteps.text: Ready-mix concrete (3,500-4,000 PSI) is poured and screeded to the planned slope. Integral color (added at the truck) or color hardener (broadcast onto the surface) is applied for through-color or top-color.

howToSteps.name: Bull-float and time for stamping
howToSteps.text: The crew bull-floats to bring cream to the top. The slab is left to firm to the right consistency for stamping — soft enough to take the imprint cleanly but firm enough to hold the pattern.

howToSteps.name: Apply release agent and stamp
howToSteps.text: Powder or liquid release agent is broadcast for antiquing color and to prevent stamps from sticking. Polyurethane stamping mats are placed in sequence and tamped to transfer the pattern across the deck.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints and detail edges
howToSteps.text: Control joints are cut at 24-30x slab thickness, hidden in the stamp pattern grout lines where possible. Edges are hand-tooled for clean lines at the pool coping and outer perimeter.

howToSteps.name: Wash, seal, and optional cool-deck
howToSteps.text: After 7-day cure, the deck is pressure-washed to remove excess release agent. An acrylic or water-based sealer is applied. If cool-deck is specified, the topcoat is sprayed or troweled over the sealed surface in 1-2 coats.

content:
Quick Answer: A stamped concrete pool deck costs $10-$22 per square foot installed in 2026. A typical 600-square-foot pool surround runs $6,000-$13,200, with most quotes between $7,500-$10,500. Broom-finish (no stamping) drops the cost to $7-$12 per square foot. Cool-deck topcoat adds $1-$3 per square foot at install or $3-$7 per square foot as standalone.

Pool decks are the most-quoted decorative concrete project in residential — a high-visibility, high-comfort surface that homeowners are willing to spend on. They are also the concrete project most commonly mispriced because pool decks combine the structural complexity of a patio with the integration requirements of a pool surround. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 stamped pool deck pricing, broom alternatives, cool-deck add-ons, and resurfacing options.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed pool-deck, patio, driveway, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a stamped concrete pool deck runs $10-$22 per square foot installed nationally. On a typical 600-square-foot pool surround that's $6,000-$13,200, with most quotes landing between $7,500-$10,500. Standard broom-finish concrete pool decks are cheaper at $7-$12 per square foot but lack the texture-and-color value most homeowners want at a pool. Cool-deck coatings (acrylic cement-based topcoats that stay 20-30 degrees cooler underfoot) add $1-$3 per square foot over the stamped baseline. Pool decks carry a higher base-prep cost than patios because they often integrate with pool coping, require expansion joints at the pool edge, and need stricter drainage to direct water away from the pool shell.

2026 pricing by deck size

National typical installed pricing for stamped concrete pool decks. Assumes a 4-inch slab, 3,500-4,000 PSI ready-mix, rebar reinforcement, single-color stamp pattern, and integrated sealer.

Pool surroundDeck sizeStamped low ($10/sf)Stamped typical ($16/sf)Stamped high ($22/sf)
Round pool, 4 ft deck~350 sq ft$3,500$5,600$7,700
18 x 36 rect, 4 ft deck~600 sq ft$6,000$9,600$13,200
18 x 36 rect, 6 ft deck~800 sq ft$8,000$12,800$17,600
16 x 32 with shallow lounge~750 sq ft$7,500$12,000$16,500
Large entertainment deck1,200 sq ft$12,000$19,200$26,400

The most common residential pool deck is the 600-800 sq ft range surrounding a 16 x 32 or 18 x 36 rectangular inground pool. At a $16/sf typical national rate, that's roughly $9,600-$12,800 in stamped concrete deck before any cool-deck topcoat or premium pattern.

Broom finish vs stamped pool deck

Broom finish is the cheapest pool deck option and the most slip-resistant. It's the default for budget-conscious installs and for decks where slip safety is the dominant design consideration. The trade is appearance — broom finish reads as utility concrete vs the textured, patterned visual of stamped.

Finish2026 cost per sf600 sq ft totalSlip resistanceLifespan
Broom finish (gray)$7-$12$4,200-$7,200Excellent30-50 years
Broom finish (colored)$8-$13$4,800-$7,800Excellent30-50 years
Stamped (single color)$10-$15$6,000-$9,000Good (texture-dependent)25-35 years
Stamped (multi-color)$13-$18$7,800-$10,800Good (texture-dependent)25-35 years
Stamped + cool-deck$13-$22$7,800-$13,200Excellent25-35 years (recoat at 5-8)
Acrylic cool-deck only$10-$15$6,000-$9,000Excellent20-30 years (recoat at 5-8)

For the full stamped-vs-broom upcharge breakdown across applications, see Cost of Concrete's stamped concrete cost guide.

Cool-deck explained

Cool-deck is the umbrella term for acrylic-cement topcoats specifically engineered to stay cooler under direct sunlight. They are sprayed or troweled in 1-2 thin coats over cured concrete and cure to a textured, slip-resistant, lighter-colored surface.

Performance numbers reported by manufacturers and confirmed in independent residential testing: a standard medium-gray concrete pool deck reaches 135-145°F in 95°F ambient full sun. The same deck with a beige or sand-colored cool-deck topcoat reaches 105-115°F under identical conditions. That's a 25-35°F drop — the difference between a deck that's barefoot-comfortable and a deck that's burning. The Portland Cement Association publishes guidance on light-colored concrete surfaces and the related urban-heat-island science.

Cool-deck pricing: $1-$3 per square foot when applied at the time of the original stamped pour (lower because crew is already mobilized), or $3-$7 per square foot as a standalone application on existing concrete. Recoating cool-deck every 5-8 years runs $2-$4 per square foot.

What's different vs a patio

A pool deck is structurally similar to a patio but carries five differences that affect cost.

Pool edge detail

The deck-to-pool-shell joint requires expansion-joint material (1/2-inch closed-cell foam typically) that allows the deck and pool to move independently with thermal cycling. This detail adds $4-$8 per linear foot of pool perimeter.

Drainage requirements

The deck must slope away from the pool at 1-2 percent and direct runoff to deck drains or a discharge point away from both the pool shell and any adjacent structures. Strict municipalities require a positive drainage plan. Drainage detail adds $1-$3 per square foot.

Slab edge and cantilever

If the deck cantilevers over the pool coping (common in clean modern designs), the slab edge requires reinforced detail. Cantilevered edges add $200-$600 to the deck cost depending on pool perimeter.

Chemical exposure

Chlorinated or salt-treated pool water contacts the deck routinely. Sealer choice matters more than at non-pool concrete — penetrating siloxane sealers are preferred to film-forming acrylics that yellow under chlorine. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, water-based low-VOC sealers are the residential standard.

Thermal cycling

Pool decks see the most aggressive thermal cycling of any residential concrete — wet-to-dry, cool-to-hot, full-sun-to-shade across daily and seasonal cycles. The slab requires correct expansion-joint placement (24-30x slab thickness, hidden in stamp pattern where possible) and proper air-entrainment (minimum 5-7 percent) in freeze-thaw climates.

How a pool deck is installed

  1. Site prep and excavation. The pool deck footprint is marked and excavated to 7-8 inches (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Any existing deck material is removed and hauled off. Adjacent landscaping and the pool itself are protected with plastic and plywood.
  2. Base preparation and forms. The subgrade is compacted with a plate compactor and a 4-inch compacted gravel base is installed. Forms are set to mark the deck edges with the proper slope (1-2 percent away from the pool). Expansion joint material is placed where the deck meets the pool coping.
  3. Place reinforcement. Rebar (#3 on 18-inch centers recommended) or wire mesh is placed mid-slab on chairs. Reinforcement at the pool edge is tied carefully to keep the slab from cracking at the edge under thermal cycling.
  4. Pour the slab and apply color. Ready-mix concrete (3,500-4,000 PSI) is poured and screeded to the planned slope. Integral color (added at the truck) or color hardener (broadcast onto the surface) is applied for through-color or top-color.
  5. Bull-float and time for stamping. The crew bull-floats to bring cream to the top. The slab is left to firm to the right consistency for stamping — soft enough to take the imprint cleanly but firm enough to hold the pattern.
  6. Apply release agent and stamp. Powder or liquid release agent is broadcast for antiquing color and to prevent stamps from sticking. Polyurethane stamping mats are placed in sequence and tamped to transfer the pattern across the deck.
  7. Cut control joints and detail edges. Control joints are cut at 24-30x slab thickness, hidden in the stamp pattern grout lines where possible. Edges are hand-tooled for clean lines at the pool coping and outer perimeter.
  8. Wash, seal, and optional cool-deck. After 7-day cure, the deck is pressure-washed to remove excess release agent. An acrylic or water-based sealer is applied. If cool-deck is specified, the topcoat is sprayed or troweled over the sealed surface in 1-2 coats.

Resurfacing an existing pool deck

If the existing pool deck is structurally sound — no settlement, no major cracking, no base failure — resurfacing is 30-60 percent cheaper than full demo-and-replace. Two main resurface methods.

Stamped overlay

A 3/8 to 1/2 inch polymer-modified concrete topping is troweled over the prepared surface, then stamped while plastic. Cost: $5-$10 per square foot. Lifespan: 10-15 years. Best for decks where the original surface is intact but visually dated.

Acrylic cool-deck topcoat

A spray-applied acrylic-cement topcoat over cleaned and primed existing concrete. Cost: $3-$7 per square foot. Lifespan: 20-30 years between full reapplication, but most owners recoat every 5-8 years to maintain color. Cheapest pool deck upgrade per square foot.

Full demo and replace

Required when slab is cracked, settled, or base has failed. Cost: $11-$25 per square foot including demolition and haul-off (similar dynamics to driveway replacement covered in Cost of Concrete's driveway replacement guide). Worth it when repair would cost more than 50-60 percent of replacement.

Regional pricing variation

Pool deck pricing varies 30-50 percent between U.S. metros. The highest pool-deck-volume markets — Florida, Texas, Arizona, Southern California — have the deepest skilled stamp-crew supply and price most competitively at $10-$14 per square foot. Markets with shorter pool seasons and shallower crew supply (Mountain West, Upper Midwest, Pacific Northwest) price 25-40 percent above the cheapest metros at $14-$22 per square foot.

For full decorative-concrete context, see Cost of Concrete's stamped and decorative concrete service hub. To compare local pool deck pricing against verified contractors, browse the national best concrete contractors directory.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a stamped concrete pool deck cost in 2026?

A stamped concrete pool deck runs $10-$22 per square foot installed in 2026. On a typical 600-square-foot pool surround (4 feet around an 18 x 36 ft rectangular pool), that's $6,000-$13,200. Most quotes come in at $7,500-$10,500. Custom patterns, multi-color hand-finishing, or cool-deck add-ons push to the upper end of the range.

Broom-finish concrete pool decks run $7-$12 per square foot installed in 2026, vs $10-$22 for stamped. On a 600-square-foot deck, broom finish is $4,200-$7,200 — typically $2,000-$5,000 cheaper than stamped. The structural slab spec is identical; you're saving on color, pattern, and finish labor by going broom.

Cost: $3-$7 per square foot as standalone application, $1-$3 added when applied at the time of the original stamped pour. According to research cited by the Portland Cement Association, light-colored cool-deck surfaces reach 105-110°F in full sun vs 135-145°F for standard gray concrete.

Yes. Pool decks are typically reinforced with either #3 rebar on 18-inch centers (recommended) or 6x6 W2.9 wire mesh (acceptable for foot-only traffic). The pool edge and any cantilevered coping require specific reinforcement that's tied into the pool shell. That's 30-60 percent cheaper than full demo and replacement. Key takeaways

  • 2026 national pricing: stamped concrete pool deck $10-$22 per square foot, broom finish $7-$12 per square foot.
  • Typical 600 sq ft pool surround: $6,000-$13,200 stamped, $4,200-$7,200 broom.
  • Cool-deck topcoat adds $1-$3 per square foot at install or $3-$7 standalone; drops surface temperature 25-35°F in full sun.
  • Pool decks need reinforcement (#3 rebar on 18-inch centers preferred over wire mesh), proper expansion joints at the pool edge, and 1-2 percent drainage slope away from the pool.
  • Resurfacing options (stamped overlay or cool-deck topcoat) save 30-60 percent vs full replacement when the underlying slab is sound.
  • Highest pool-volume markets (Florida, Texas, Arizona, SoCal) price most competitively because of deeper skilled-crew supply.

Need a contractor whose pool deck quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local pool deck pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
